h1Title: Sciatica: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Sciatica describes radiating pain that travels from the lower back through the buttock and down one leg along the path of the sciatic nerve, the longest and widest nerve in the human body. Roughly 5-10% of adults with low back pain have true sciatica, with lifetime prevalence estimated at 10-40% in population-based studies. The pain typically follows a dermatomal distribution — L4, L5, or S1 — and ranges from a dull ache to sharp, burning, or electric sensations. A herniated lumbar disc compresses the nerve root in roughly 90% of cases. Most episodes resolve within 6-12 weeks with conservative management; persistent or worsening neurological deficits warrant imaging and specialist review.

keyStats.prevalence: Lifetime prevalence 10-40% in population studies; active sciatica affects roughly 2-3% of the general population at any time
keyStats.demographics: Peak incidence age 45-64; men slightly more affected than women; physically demanding occupations carry 2-3× higher risk
keyStats.avgAge: Most common onset in the fourth and fifth decades of life
keyStats.globalCases: Sciatica is among the leading causes of work disability globally; exact global case counts are not tracked separately from low-back-pain statistics

definition: Sciatica (ICD-10: M54.3-M54.4) is a radicular pain syndrome caused by irritation, compression, or inflammation of one or more lumbosacral nerve roots that form the sciatic nerve (L4-S3). The sciatic nerve exits the pelvis through the greater sciatic foramen, passes beneath or through the piriformis muscle, and travels down the posterior thigh to the knee where it divides into the tibial and common peroneal branches. Radicular pain follows specific dermatomal patterns: L4 compression causes medial calf and foot pain; L5 causes lateral calf and dorsal foot pain; S1 causes posterior calf and sole pain. Disc herniation at L4-L5 or L5-S1 causes 90% of cases by directly compressing a nerve root or inducing inflammatory cytokine release. Other causes include lumbar spinal stenosis, spondylolisthesis, piriformis syndrome, foraminal stenosis, and rarely epidural abscess or tumour. The term sciatica is a symptom descriptor rather than a diagnosis; identifying the underlying structural pathology is essential for targeted treatment and prognosis. Orthopedic surgeons, neurosurgeons, physiatrists, and pain medicine specialists share responsibility for management depending on severity and underlying cause.

typesClassification.description: A herniated nucleus pulposus at L4-L5 or L5-S1 compresses the exiting nerve root, causing acute radicular pain and potentially motor weakness or sensory loss. Accounts for roughly 90% of sciatica cases. Most resolve within 6-12 weeks without surgery.

Narrowing of the lumbar spinal canal or neural foramina, usually from degenerative osteophytes and ligamentum flavum hypertrophy, compresses one or multiple nerve roots. Pain typically worsens with walking and standing and is relieved by flexion. Most common in patients over 60.

External compression of the sciatic nerve by a hypertrophied or spasmed piriformis muscle in the deep buttock. Accounts for less than 10% of cases. Diagnosed by exclusion of disc and canal pathology; responds to physiotherapy and, if needed, botulinum toxin injection.
Forward slippage of one vertebral body over another narrows the foramen and stretches the exiting nerve root. Degenerative spondylolisthesis at L4-L5 is the most common variant in older adults.

primarySymptoms: Sharp, shooting, burning, or electric pain originating in the lower back or buttock and radiating down one leg, typically to the calf or foot, following the course of the sciatic nerve.
Pain that worsens with sitting, coughing, sneezing, or the Valsalva maneuver — all of which raise intraspinal pressure and compress the affected nerve root.
Numbness or tingling (paraesthesia) in a dermatomal distribution: medial calf and arch of foot in L4; dorsum of foot and great-toe area in L5; lateral foot, heel, and small toes in S1.
Weakness in specific muscle groups: ankle dorsiflexion weakness (foot drop tendency) with L4 or L5 compression; plantar flexion and toe flexion weakness with S1.
Unilateral leg pain that is typically worse than the accompanying back pain — a distinguishing feature of radiculopathy versus mechanical low-back pain.
Pain relief when lying flat with the hips and knees flexed (fetal position), which reduces foraminal compression and intradiscal pressure.
Buttock pain and deep posterior thigh aching that may be the primary complaint in piriformis syndrome or high-level disc pathology.
Reduced or absent ankle reflexes (S1), reduced knee reflex (L4), or reduced great-toe extension strength — detectable on clinical examination.
Night pain that interrupts sleep in more severe cases, particularly when disc herniation is large or inflammatory response is significant.
Bilateral leg symptoms, saddle anaesthesia, or bowel and bladder dysfunction — rare but constitute red flags for cauda equina syndrome.
earlyWarningSigns: Unilateral buttock or posterior thigh ache developing over days following heavy lifting, prolonged sitting, or trunk rotation — suggests early foraminal nerve root irritation before full radicular pain develops.
Mild intermittent foot or calf tingling provoked only by prolonged sitting or specific postures — early radicular sensitisation without constant pain.
Subtle difficulty standing on tip-toe on one foot (S1) or on heels (L4/L5) — early motor involvement that patients often attribute to fatigue.
emergencySigns: New onset of urinary retention, faecal incontinence, or bilateral lower limb weakness — these indicate cauda equina syndrome, a surgical emergency requiring MRI within 4 hours.
Rapidly progressive unilateral foot drop or complete lower limb weakness developing over hours to days — urgent neuroimaging and orthopaedic or neurosurgical assessment within 24 hours.
Severe progressive pain unresponsive to all analgesia, fever, and night sweats — raises concern for epidural abscess or metastatic spinal disease; warrants emergency MRI.

causes.description: Rupture of the annulus fibrosus allows the nucleus pulposus to protrude posterolaterally, directly compressing the adjacent nerve root or triggering release of phospholipase A2 and inflammatory cytokines. L4-L5 and L5-S1 are the most commonly affected levels, together accounting for over 90% of discogenic sciatica.

Age-related hypertrophy of facet joints, osteophyte formation, and ligamentum flavum thickening narrow the spinal canal and intervertebral foramina. Walking and lumbar extension worsen nerve-root ischaemia; flexion (bending forward, sitting) provides relief. Most common cause of sciatica in patients over 60.

Facet-joint degeneration allows one vertebral body to slip forward relative to the one below, narrowing the foramen and stretching the exiting nerve root. Grade I-II spondylolisthesis at L4-L5 is the most common variant, predominantly affecting women over 50.

The piriformis muscle in the deep buttock can compress the sciatic nerve when hypertrophied, in spasm, or anomalously divided around the nerve. Triggered by hip overuse, trauma, or prolonged sitting. Causes roughly 6-8% of sciatica cases in some series.

causes.cause: Foraminal stenosis from osteophytes or disc-height loss
causes.description: Chronic disc desiccation reduces vertebral height, causing the neural foramen to narrow concentrically. Combined osteophyte and soft-tissue crowding compresses the dorsal root ganglion and exiting nerve root, producing a pattern of chronic, positional radicular pain.

Epidural metastases (breast, lung, prostate, kidney), epidural haematoma (especially in anticoagulated patients), and epidural abscess (from intravenous drug use or spinal procedures) can all mimic discogenic sciatica but progress rapidly and require emergency imaging.

riskFactors.description: Intervertebral disc degeneration accelerates after 40, with peak disc-herniation incidence in the fourth and fifth decades. Spinal stenosis peaks after 60.
Manual handling, prolonged heavy lifting, and repetitive trunk flexion-rotation increase disc herniation risk roughly 2-3× compared with sedentary occupations (GBD 2019 occupational risk data).
Sitting at 90° increases intradiscal pressure at L3-L4 and L4-L5 by roughly 40% compared with standing. Office workers and professional drivers have elevated sciatica incidence.
Each unit increase in BMI above 25 increases lumbar disc degeneration risk; obesity correlates with earlier onset of disc herniation and reduced recovery rates from conservative treatment.
Nicotine impairs disc nutrition through avascular diffusion and accelerates nucleus pulposus desiccation. Smokers have a 1.5-2× higher risk of intervertebral disc degeneration in cohort studies.
Deconditioning of the lumbar multifidus and core musculature reduces spinal stability and increases disc load during movement, raising herniation risk.
A history of lumbar disc injury or previous laminectomy alters spinal biomechanics and increases the likelihood of recurrent disc herniation or adjacent-segment disease.

affectedDemographics: Peak incidence in adults aged 45-64 years (Konstantinou & Dunn, Spine 2008).
Men aged 25-54 have slightly higher incidence than women due to greater occupational physical load; after 60, women develop more stenotic sciatica.
Occupational groups: drivers, construction workers, nurses, and warehouse staff have 2-3× higher prevalence than white-collar workers.
Prevalence is highest in high-income countries with seated occupational cultures combined with physical labour; rural subsistence-farming populations have different but still elevated patterns.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis relies primarily on history and physical examination. A radicular pain pattern — buttock pain radiating below the knee in a dermatomal distribution — combined with a positive straight-leg raise (SLR) test provides roughly 80% sensitivity for L4-L5 or L5-S1 disc herniation. The SLR is performed with the patient supine: passive elevation of the ipsilateral leg to 30-70° that reproduces the radiating pain is positive. The cross-SLR (contralateral leg elevation reproduces ipsilateral pain) is less sensitive but more specific for large central disc herniation. Neurological examination documents motor strength, reflexes, and sensory changes to map the affected root level. Imaging is not required for typical acute sciatica within the first 6 weeks unless red flags are present (fever, unexplained weight loss, progressive neurological deficit, history of cancer, or immunosuppression). MRI of the lumbar spine is the preferred imaging modality when indicated, as it identifies disc herniation, foraminal stenosis, and soft-tissue lesions without radiation. CT is used when MRI is contraindicated or to evaluate bony osteophytes and foraminal dimensions. EMG and nerve conduction studies quantify axonal loss and chronicity but rarely change acute management. Electromyography is most useful in atypical presentations, bilateral symptoms, and pre-operative planning.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Clinical provocation test — positive result (pain below knee at 30-70° of passive hip flexion) has ~80% sensitivity for L4-S1 disc herniation
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed in the office. You lie flat on your back while the examiner slowly lifts your straight leg. Reproduction of your radiating leg pain (not just back pain) at less than 70° is a positive result. Painless or hamstring-tightness-only is negative.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies disc herniation, nerve root compression, spinal stenosis, spondylolisthesis, or other structural pathology
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Lie flat in a tube-shaped scanner for 30-45 minutes. No radiation. A contrast dye (gadolinium) may be injected via a hand vein for post-surgical spines. Results available within 24-48 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates bony canal dimensions, osteophytes, and facet arthropathy; preferred post-surgically or when MRI is contraindicated
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 5-10 minute scan lying still on a table. Lower-dose radiation than plain X-ray series. Results same day in most centres.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Maps the affected nerve root by testing motor power, deep tendon reflexes, and dermatomal sensation
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Bedside clinical exam, 10-15 minutes. The examiner tests strength (heel walking, toe walking), taps tendons for reflexes, and tests sensation with a pin or monofilament. No equipment beyond a reflex hammer and pin.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects axonal loss, distinguishes radiculopathy from peripheral neuropathy, and estimates chronicity
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Thin electrodes are placed in specific muscles and small electrical pulses applied to skin. Mild discomfort. The test takes 60-90 minutes and is most useful when imaging and symptoms disagree or when surgical planning requires precise level localisation.

treatmentOverview: Most sciatica resolves without surgery: approximately 60-70% of patients improve within 6 weeks and 90% within 12 weeks with conservative management (Peul et al. NEJM 2007). NICE guideline NG59 and the American College of Physicians (ACP 2017) recommend an active approach centred on early physiotherapy, staying mobile, and targeted analgesia rather than bed rest. First-line analgesia uses NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400-600 mg three times daily, or naproxen 500 mg twice daily) for up to 4 weeks, with paracetamol as adjunct. Oral corticosteroids (methylprednisolone dose pack or prednisone 40-60 mg/day for 5 days) reduce inflammatory oedema around the nerve root and provide short-term pain relief in acute severe presentations, though effects on long-term recovery are modest. Physiotherapy includes neural mobilisation, McKenzie directional exercises, and core stabilisation; evidence shows comparable outcomes to surgery at 12 months for most patients with disc herniation. Epidural corticosteroid injections (ESI) — transforaminal, interlaminar, or caudal — reduce radicular pain and improve function in the short term (2-6 weeks) and are most useful as a bridge to physiotherapy or to defer surgery. Neuropathic analgesics (gabapentin 300-1200 mg/day or pregabalin 75-150 mg twice daily) address the burning, electric component of radicular pain. Surgery (microdiscectomy or laminectomy) is reserved for patients with cauda equina syndrome, progressive neurological deficit, or failure of 6-12 weeks of conservative treatment. When indicated, microdiscectomy provides faster pain relief than continued conservative care at 3 months, though outcomes converge by 12 months.

medicalTreatments.name: NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400-600 mg three times daily, naproxen 500 mg twice daily)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line analgesia for acute sciatica. Reduces prostaglandin-mediated inflammation around the compressed nerve root. Taken with food to reduce GI side-effects; use the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Moderate pain relief (NRS reduction ~2 points) versus placebo in RCTs; most effective in the first 4 weeks of acute episodes

medicalTreatments.name: Oral corticosteroids (methylprednisolone dose pack or prednisone 40-60 mg/day for 5-7 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Short-course systemic steroids reduce perineural inflammatory oedema acutely. Most useful in the first 2 weeks of severe radicular pain. Blood-glucose monitoring required in diabetic patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Significant short-term pain reduction and improved function at 3 weeks versus placebo in one large RCT (Goldberg et al. JAMA 2015); minimal 1-year benefit

medicalTreatments.name: Gabapentin (300-1,200 mg/day in divided doses)
medicalTreatments.description: Alpha-2-delta calcium channel ligand that reduces ectopic nerve discharge and central sensitisation. Used for the burning, tingling, and electric components of radicular pain when NSAIDs are insufficient.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest benefit over placebo for neuropathic radicular pain; Cochrane review 2019 found low-quality evidence; sedation is the primary dose-limiting side-effect

medicalTreatments.name: Epidural corticosteroid injection (ESI: transforaminal or interlaminar)
medicalTreatments.description: Corticosteroid (methylprednisolone 40-80 mg) with local anaesthetic deposited at the nerve root under fluoroscopic or CT guidance. Reduces inflammatory mediators around the disc-nerve interface.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Significant reduction in radicular pain at 2-6 weeks in ~50-60% of patients; does not reduce the rate of eventual surgery at 12 months (Friedly et al. NEJM 2014)

medicalTreatments.name: Physiotherapy — directional exercises and neural mobilisation
medicalTreatments.description: McKenzie repeated end-range directional exercises, core stabilisation, and nerve-flossing techniques reduce disc nuclear migration and desensitise the nerve root. Evidence supports early active rehabilitation over passive rest.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Equivalent to surgical outcomes at 12 months for most disc herniations; combination with medication superior to either alone at 6 weeks

surgicalOptions.name: Microdiscectomy (microscopic lumbar discectomy)
surgicalOptions.description: Minimally invasive removal of the herniated disc fragment through a 1.5-2 cm incision using microsurgical technique. Same-day or overnight admission. Performed under general anaesthesia. Nerve root decompression is immediate.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 80-90% report excellent or good leg-pain relief; faster recovery of leg pain versus conservative care at 3 months, with equivalent outcomes at 12 months (SPORT trial, NEJM 2006)

surgicalOptions.name: Laminectomy / laminotomy for spinal stenosis
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical widening of the bony canal and removal of hypertrophied ligamentum flavum to decompress multiple nerve roots. Used for stenotic sciatica that fails conservative management. May be combined with fusion when spondylolisthesis is present.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 70-85% report durable improvement at 2 years; stenosis-related symptoms recur in roughly 20% by 10 years

alternativeTreatments.name: Acupuncture
alternativeTreatments.description: Several RCTs and a 2015 Cochrane review found acupuncture provides modest short-term pain relief versus sham in non-specific low-back pain with radicular features; effect sizes are small and evidence specifically for sciatica is limited.

alternativeTreatments.name: Spinal manipulation (chiropractic / osteopathic)
alternativeTreatments.description: High-velocity low-amplitude thrust techniques may reduce radicular pain in acute disc herniation; ACP 2017 guideline includes spinal manipulation as an option for acute/subacute low-back pain. Contraindicated with progressive neurological deficit or cauda equina signs.

whySeeSpecialist: Orthopaedic or neurosurgical assessment is indicated when sciatica causes progressive motor weakness, fails 6-12 weeks of conservative management, or presents with cauda equina red flags. Pain medicine specialists offer image-guided epidural injections when physiotherapy and oral medication have not controlled radicular pain within 4-6 weeks.
doctorSelectionGuide: For uncomplicated acute sciatica, a GP or physiotherapist with MSK experience can manage the first 6 weeks. If symptoms persist, seek an orthopaedic surgeon, neurosurgeon, or spinal physiatrist with documented experience in lumbar disc pathology and minimally invasive surgery. Verify the surgeon's annual discectomy volume — centres performing more than 100 procedures per year have better outcomes.
hospitalCriteria: MRI capability for lumbar spine within 24 hours for suspected cauda equina syndrome
Fluoroscopy or CT-guided injection suite for epidural steroid injections
Orthopaedic or neurosurgical unit with microsurgical instrumentation
Physiotherapy department with certified spinal physiotherapists
Neuromonitoring capability for complex spinal decompression
keyFacilities: Tertiary spine centres with combined surgical and non-surgical MDT
Interventional pain management clinics for ESI and nerve blocks
Outpatient MSK physiotherapy practices with McKenzie-certified therapists
Orthopaedic urgent care or same-day spinal assessment services
preventionStrategies: Maintain a healthy body weight — BMI reduction below 25 decreases disc load and inflammatory cytokine levels that accelerate disc degeneration.
Use correct manual-handling technique at work: lift with the legs, keep loads close to the body, avoid simultaneous trunk flexion and rotation.
Break prolonged sitting every 30-45 minutes with brief walks or stretches — intradiscal pressure normalises within 60 seconds of standing.
Strengthen the lumbar multifidus and core with progressive resistance training at least twice weekly.
Stop smoking — cohort data show a 30-50% reduction in disc degeneration rate in long-term ex-smokers versus current smokers.
Ergonomic workstation setup: chair lumbar support, screen at eye level, and avoiding a forward-head posture that loads the lumbar spine compensatorily.
lifestyleModifications: Stay as active as pain allows — bed rest beyond 48 hours is associated with slower recovery and more disability than continued gentle movement.
Apply heat (warm compress or heat pad) to the lower back for 15-20 minutes three times daily to reduce muscle spasm around the irritated nerve root.
Try ice during the first 48-72 hours of acute flare to limit perineural inflammatory oedema.
Sleep with a pillow between the knees when lying on one side, or with a rolled towel under the lumbar spine when supine, to reduce disc pressure.
Avoid long periods in a car or plane without stopping — static nerve root compression in a flexed-seated posture worsens radicular symptoms.
Perform prescribed physiotherapy exercises daily, even on pain-free days, to prevent deconditioning and recurrence.

recommended: Anti-inflammatory foods (oily fish, walnuts, olive oil) to support nerve-root healing and reduce systemic inflammation
Adequate protein (1.2 g/kg/day) to maintain paravertebral muscle mass during relative rest
Calcium and vitamin D (from dairy, fortified foods, or supplements) to maintain bone density at the vertebrae
Fruits and vegetables rich in antioxidants to reduce oxidative stress in degenerate disc tissue
avoid: Excess refined carbohydrates and ultra-processed foods, which elevate systemic inflammatory markers and accelerate disc degeneration
High-dose alcohol, which impairs nerve recovery and interacts with gabapentinoids and NSAIDs
Excess caffeine at night, which disrupts restorative sleep needed for nerve-root healing

exerciseGuidelines: Walking is the safest and most evidence-supported exercise for acute sciatica — 20-30 minutes daily at comfortable pace is the primary recommendation. McKenzie directional extension exercises reduce disc nuclear migration toward the nerve root in most patients. Swimming or aquatic physiotherapy offloads disc pressure. Heavy axial loading (barbell squats, deadlifts) should be avoided until symptoms resolve. Return to full sport and occupational lifting is guided by absence of neurological signs and completion of a structured rehabilitation programme.
dailyManagement: Take prescribed NSAIDs or neuropathic pain medication at regular intervals (not only on breakthrough pain) to maintain steady-state anti-inflammatory effect.
Complete a 15-minute morning stretching and McKenzie exercise routine before activity.
Pace daily activity — identify the positions and tasks that provoke radicular symptoms and modify or limit them, especially in the first 4-6 weeks.
Use a lumbar support cushion during any unavoidable prolonged sitting.
Keep a symptom diary (pain score, areas of tingling, any weakness) to track progress and report accurately at follow-up appointments.
prognosis: Prognosis is generally favourable for acute sciatica from disc herniation. In a landmark RCT (SPORT Trial, NEJM 2006), 90% of patients reported substantial improvement within 12 months regardless of surgical or conservative pathway. Patients treated surgically recovered faster on the leg pain dimension at 3 months. Recurrent disc herniation occurs in approximately 7-10% within 10 years of microdiscectomy. Stenotic sciatica in older adults tends to follow a slower recovery trajectory; 60-75% improve sufficiently with physiotherapy and injection therapy to avoid surgery, though symptoms often fluctuate. Poor prognostic factors include severe motor deficit at presentation, symptom duration exceeding 3 months before treatment, central sensitisation, and high catastrophising scores on psychometric testing. Cauda equina syndrome, if decompressed within 24-48 hours, has good bladder and bowel recovery rates; delays beyond 48 hours are associated with permanent sphincter dysfunction in roughly 30% of cases.
recoveryTimeline: Acute disc-related sciatica: 60-70% improve within 6 weeks; 90% within 12 weeks with conservative care. After microdiscectomy: leg pain resolves within 2-6 weeks in 80-90% of patients; return to desk work at 2-4 weeks, physical work at 6-8 weeks. Spinal stenosis: slower improvement over 3-6 months of rehabilitation. Cauda equina syndrome: sphincter recovery most likely if surgically decompressed within 24 hours.
complications: Permanent motor weakness or foot drop if nerve-root compression is prolonged and severe — annual incidence of foot drop from disc herniation is approximately 0.5-1 per 10,000 adults.
Cauda equina syndrome with permanent bladder or bowel dysfunction — occurs in roughly 1-2% of lumbar disc herniations presenting with bilateral leg symptoms; requires emergency surgery.

Chronic radicular pain and central sensitisation after 3-6 months of unresolved acute sciatica, with psychological comorbidity (depression, fear-avoidance) driving disability beyond the structural lesion.
Post-operative complications of microdiscectomy: recurrent herniation (7-10%), discitis or wound infection (1-2%), dural tear with CSF leak (1-3%).
Iatrogenic adjacent-segment degeneration after spinal fusion, leading to new radiculopathy at the level above or below the fused segment within 5-10 years.

supportResources.name: NICE Back Pain Guidelines (NG59)
supportResources.description: UK evidence-based guideline for low-back pain and sciatica including treatment pathway recommendations.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Orthopaedic Surgeons — Sciatica
supportResources.description: Patient-facing orthopaedic society information on sciatica, treatment options, and when to seek surgery.

supportResources.name: Arthritis Research UK — Back Pain Resources
supportResources.description: Evidence-based patient resource covering sciatica, exercise guidance, and self-management.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lumbar radiculopathy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Sciatica is a colloquial term for sciatic nerve pain; lumbar radiculopathy is the clinical diagnosis encompassing nerve-root irritation at L1-S1. All sciatica is lumbar radiculopathy, but radiculopathy can occur at L1-L3 producing groin or anterior-thigh pain without the posterior-leg radiation typical of sciatica.

confusedWithConditions.name: Mechanical low back pain
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Mechanical back pain is localised to the lumbar region and does not radiate below the knee. Sciatica typically produces leg pain worse than back pain, following a dermatomal pattern, and has a positive straight-leg raise. Distinguishing both on clinical exam changes the management pathway.

confusedWithConditions.name: Peripheral neuropathy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Peripheral neuropathy causes bilateral stocking-pattern numbness and burning, typically worse distally. Sciatica is unilateral, follows a dermatomal stripe, correlates with a structural lumbar lesion on imaging, and has a positive SLR. Nerve conduction studies distinguish the two when clinical presentation overlaps.

confusedWithConditions.name: Herniated disc
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A herniated disc is the most common structural cause of sciatica. Disc herniation without nerve-root compression can cause local back pain without radicular features. Sciatica requires both the disc lesion and demonstrable nerve-root involvement — indicated by dermatomal pain, reflex changes, or weakness.

relatedConditions.name: Lumbar radiculopathy
relatedConditions.relevance: Sciatica is the lay term for lumbar radiculopathy involving the L4-S1 roots; the two terms are clinically interchangeable in most cases.

relatedConditions.name: Spinal stenosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Degenerative spinal stenosis is the leading cause of sciatica in adults over 60, producing neurogenic claudication as the dominant symptom.

relatedConditions.name: Herniated disc
relatedConditions.relevance: Lumbar disc herniation is the most common cause of sciatica, and most surgical discussions about sciatica centre on disc-related pathology.

relatedConditions.name: Cervical radiculopathy
relatedConditions.relevance: Analogous nerve-root compression syndrome in the cervical spine; same diagnostic and conservative management principles apply.

relatedConditions.name: Failed back surgery syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Persistent or recurrent radicular pain after lumbar surgery — a recognised complication pathway from surgically treated sciatica.

faqs.question: What is sciatica and what causes it?
faqs.answer: Sciatica is radiating pain along the sciatic nerve from the lower back through the buttock and down one leg. It is caused by compression or irritation of a lumbar nerve root — most often by a herniated disc at L4-L5 or L5-S1. Other causes include spinal stenosis, spondylolisthesis, and piriformis syndrome.

faqs.question: How do I know if I have sciatica or just back pain?
faqs.answer: Sciatica typically produces radiating pain below the knee following a specific dermatomal stripe, with associated tingling or numbness. Pure back pain stays in the lumbar region without leg radiation. A positive straight-leg raise test — pain below the knee when the examiner lifts your straight leg — strongly suggests sciatica rather than mechanical back pain.

faqs.question: How long does sciatica last?
faqs.answer: Acute disc-related sciatica resolves within 6 weeks in 60-70% of patients and within 12 weeks in roughly 90% with conservative treatment. Sciatica from spinal stenosis in older adults follows a slower course. Symptoms persisting beyond 12 weeks warrant specialist review and imaging.

faqs.question: What is the fastest way to relieve sciatica pain?
faqs.answer: Short-term relief comes from NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen), staying gently mobile, and applying heat to the lower back. A 5-7 day course of oral prednisone provides the fastest anti-inflammatory effect in severe acute cases. Epidural corticosteroid injections offer significant relief within days in 50-60% of patients when oral therapy fails.

faqs.question: Does sciatica require surgery?
faqs.answer: Surgery is needed in fewer than 10% of sciatica cases. Microdiscectomy is indicated for cauda equina syndrome, rapidly progressive motor deficit, or failure of 6-12 weeks of conservative management. At 12 months, outcomes of surgery versus continued conservative care are broadly equivalent for non-emergency cases.

faqs.question: What exercises help sciatica?
faqs.answer: McKenzie directional extension exercises, nerve-flossing (neural mobilisation), and gentle walking 20-30 minutes daily are first-line physiotherapy for disc-related sciatica. Core strengthening and lumbar stabilisation exercises are introduced once acute pain settles. High-impact exercises and heavy axial loading should be avoided during active radicular pain.

faqs.question: Can sciatica go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Yes. Roughly 60-70% of acute disc-related sciatica resolves spontaneously within 6 weeks without surgery or injections, driven by resorption of the herniated disc material and reduction of perineural inflammation. Rest alone is not recommended; staying active speeds recovery.

faqs.question: Is bed rest recommended for sciatica?
faqs.answer: No. Current guidelines (NICE NG59, ACP 2017) recommend staying as active as pain allows. Bed rest beyond 48 hours is associated with slower recovery and more prolonged disability compared with early gentle mobilisation.

faqs.question: What imaging tests diagnose sciatica?
faqs.answer: MRI of the lumbar spine is the preferred imaging test — it identifies disc herniation, canal stenosis, and soft-tissue lesions. Imaging is not needed for typical acute sciatica in the first 6 weeks unless red flags exist. CT is used when MRI is contraindicated or to assess bony anatomy.

faqs.question: What nerve is affected in sciatica?
faqs.answer: The sciatic nerve, formed from nerve roots L4-S3 in the lumbar and sacral spine. In most cases, a single root — L4, L5, or S1 — is compressed by a herniated disc, producing dermatomal pain, specific reflex changes, and focussed muscle weakness corresponding to that root level.

faqs.question: Can sciatica cause permanent nerve damage?
faqs.answer: Permanent nerve damage is uncommon but possible with prolonged severe compression. Motor weakness (foot drop) and sensory loss can become permanent if decompression is delayed. Cauda equina syndrome causes permanent bladder and bowel dysfunction in roughly 30% of cases when surgical decompression is delayed beyond 48 hours.

faqs.question: What is the difference between sciatica and a herniated disc?
faqs.answer: A herniated disc is the most common cause of sciatica. The disc is the structural lesion; sciatica is the resulting nerve-root pain syndrome. Disc herniation without nerve-root compression causes local back pain without the radiating leg symptoms that define sciatica.

faqs.question: Can sitting too long cause sciatica?
faqs.answer: Prolonged sitting increases intradiscal pressure and can precipitate disc herniation or worsen existing nerve-root compression. Office workers and professional drivers have elevated sciatica incidence. Breaking sitting every 30-45 minutes with brief walks reduces this pressure.

faqs.question: Is an epidural injection safe for sciatica?
faqs.answer: Fluoroscopy-guided epidural corticosteroid injections are safe when performed by trained interventional pain physicians, with a serious adverse event rate below 1 in 1,000. Common side-effects are mild: transient headache, flushing, or blood-glucose elevation. The benefit is primarily short-term pain reduction rather than long-term structural change.

faqs.question: When is sciatica a medical emergency?
faqs.answer: Sciatica becomes a medical emergency when it is associated with urinary retention, faecal incontinence, bilateral leg weakness, or saddle anaesthesia — signs of cauda equina syndrome. This requires MRI within 4 hours and emergency surgical decompression to prevent permanent sphincter dysfunction.

metaDescription: Sciatica: nerve pain radiating from the lower back down the leg, often from a disc. Symptoms, causes, diagnosis and treatment reviewed here.

sources.title: NICE Guideline NG59. 2016 (updated 2024). Low back pain and sciatica in over 16s: assessment and management. National Institute for Health and Care Excellence, London.
